specification.topic-statement: The agent fork operation creates a child agent that inherits the parent's conversation history, configuration, and display output, then switches the user's focus to the child.

scope.in-scope: The two fork entry points (slash command and internal tool), their argument formats, the sequence of state initialization and inheritance, the transaction scope, concurrency control, and the differences between the two entry points.
scope.boundary: How the user navigates between agents after forking (by sibling, parent, or child) is covered by the agent process tree and is out of scope here.
What happens after a prompt is submitted to the LLM — the HTTP request, response streaming, and state transitions — is out of scope.

field.name: model override
field.note: optional; format MODEL or MODEL/THINKING_LEVEL; if absent, provider, model, and thinking level are inherited from the parent

field.name: prompt
field.note: optional; must be double-quoted if present; submitted to the LLM on the child immediately after forking

field.name: name
field.note: required; a human-readable label for the child agent

field.name: prompt
field.note: required; submitted to the LLM on the child immediately after the fork completes

behavior.slash-command-fork:
The slash command fork is user-initiated. The sequence of operations is:
Before any allocation, the current agent is checked for running tool execution threads. If any are running, the command polls with a 10 ms sleep until all threads complete.
A process-wide atomic flag prevents concurrent fork operations. If the flag is already set, the command displays an error and returns without taking any action. Otherwise the flag is set.
Arguments are parsed to extract an optional `--model` flag and an optional double-quoted prompt string. Either may appear before or after the other. Unquoted text that is not a flag is an error. An unclosed quote is an error. The `--model` flag requires a value; omitting the value is an error.
The model value has the form `MODEL` or `MODEL/THINKING_LEVEL`. Valid thinking levels are `min`, `low`, `med`, and `high`. An unrecognized model name or thinking level is an error.
A database transaction is opened. All subsequent database operations (message ID query, agent insert, fork event insert) execute within this transaction. If any database operation fails, the transaction is rolled back and an error is returned.
A new agent is created with a freshly generated UUID, the current time as its creation timestamp, and the parent's UUID as its parent reference. The fork message ID is set to the parent's last persisted message ID, which marks the point of divergence.
All display layers (banner, scrollback, spinner, separator, input buffer, completion, status) are initialized fresh. A new database connection is opened for the child's worker thread. A new token cache is initialized with the configured token budget.
If a model override was supplied, the child's provider is inferred from the model name, and the model and thinking level are set from the override. If the thinking level portion was omitted from the override, the parent's thinking level is used.
If no override was supplied, the child inherits the parent's provider, model, and thinking level verbatim.
If the child's thinking level is non-minimum and the model is known not to support thinking, a warning is appended to the child's display output. The fork proceeds regardless.
The following state is copied from the parent to the child:
- **Conversation messages**: all messages with all content blocks are deep-copied. The child's copy is fully independent; subsequent changes to either agent's messages do not affect the other. - **Scrollback display output**: the visual rendering history (all lines and their layout data) is copied. - **Pinned document paths**: the array of pinned document paths is copied. - **Toolset filter**: the array of tool name restrictions is copied.
The following state is not inherited and starts fresh: input buffer, spinner state, completion state, cursor position, history navigation state, marks.
The child agent record is inserted into the database. Two fork event messages are inserted: one on the parent (kind `fork`, role `parent`, referencing the child UUID) and one on the child (kind `fork`, role `child`, referencing the parent UUID, the fork message ID, the pinned paths, and the toolset filter). The transaction is committed.
The process-wide fork flag is cleared. The REPL focus switches to the child. A confirmation message is appended to the child's display output.
If a prompt was supplied, it is submitted as a user message on the child and an LLM request is initiated.
The database transaction commits before any LLM request is started. If the LLM request fails after the fork, the fork itself remains in the database and the child agent exists. The two operations are not atomic with respect to each other.
behavior.internal-tool-fork:
The internal tool fork is initiated by the LLM. It runs on a tool execution thread, not on the main event loop thread.
A new agent is created with a freshly generated UUID. The name and prompt are taken from the JSON arguments. A `clear` event is inserted into the child's message history to signal that the child starts from a blank context (no inherited conversation).
Unlike the slash command fork, the internal tool fork does not copy conversation messages, scrollback output, pinned paths, or toolset filter. The child starts as an empty agent with only the supplied prompt.
The model, provider, and thinking level are inherited from the parent.
When the tool execution thread finishes, the REPL event loop polls the result. The child agent is transferred to the REPL's agent array. The REPL focus does not switch automatically; the user must navigate to the child manually.
The tool result returned to the LLM contains the child's UUID and name.
If adding the child to the in-memory agent array fails, the child exists in the database but is not accessible in the current session. No error is propagated to the caller.
behavior.differences-between-entry-points:
The two fork entry points differ in the following ways:
| Aspect | Slash command | Internal tool | |--------|--------------|---------------| | Initiator | User | LLM | | Thread | Event loop (main) | Tool execution thread | | Concurrency control | Process-wide atomic flag | None | | Conversation inheritance | Full deep copy | None (blank slate) | | Scrollback inheritance | Yes | No | | Pinned paths inheritance | Yes | No | | Toolset filter inheritance | Yes | No | | Model override | Supported via `--model` | Not supported; always inherits | | Child name | Not set | Required field | | Focus switch | Automatic | Manual | | Database transaction | Yes | No | | Fork event messages | Parent and child events | None | | Prompt execution | Optional | Mandatory |
behavior.concurrency-control: The process-wide fork-pending flag applies only to the slash command entry point. It prevents two concurrent slash command forks but does not prevent an internal tool fork from running concurrently. The flag is an atomic boolean stored on the shared context and is thus visible across all agents.
